questions.question: What is the primary goal of CPU scheduling in an operating system?
questions.option_a: A. To minimize the number of processes in memory
questions.option_b: B. To maximize CPU utilization by keeping the CPU as busy as possible
questions.option_c: C. To ensure all processes complete at the same time
questions.option_d: D. To eliminate I/O burst periods entirely
questions.explanation: CPU scheduling aims to maximize CPU utilization by efficiently allocating the CPU to processes from the ready queue, ensuring the processor remains busy.

questions.question: Which component is responsible for transferring control of the CPU from one process to another?
questions.option_c: C. Dispatcher
questions.option_d: D. Memory manager
questions.explanation: The dispatcher performs context switching, switches to user mode, and restarts the new user program to transfer CPU control between processes.

questions.question: What is dispatch latency?
questions.option_a: A. Time taken for a process to move from ready to waiting state
questions.option_b: B. Time taken for the dispatcher to stop one process and start another
questions.option_c: C. Time a process spends waiting in the ready queue
questions.option_d: D. Time required to complete a CPU burst
questions.explanation: Dispatch latency is the overhead time the dispatcher requires to perform a context switch and start executing the next process.

questions.question: In preemptive scheduling, when can a running process be suspended?
questions.option_a: A. Only when it completes its entire execution
questions.option_b: B. Only during I/O burst periods
questions.option_c: C. At any point during its execution
questions.option_d: D. Only when a higher priority process terminates
questions.explanation: Preemptive scheduling allows the OS to suspend a running process at any point to allocate the CPU to another process, enabling better responsiveness.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a valid trigger for CPU scheduling decisions?
questions.option_a: A. Process switches from running to waiting state
questions.option_b: B. Process switches from running to ready state
questions.option_c: C. Process switches from waiting to ready state
questions.option_d: D. Process remains in the running state without interruption
questions.explanation: Scheduling decisions occur during state transitions; if a process continues running uninterrupted, no scheduling decision is needed.

questions.question: What does 'throughput' measure in scheduling criteria?
questions.option_a: A. Time taken to execute a particular process
questions.option_b: B. Number of processes completing execution per time unit
questions.option_c: C. Time a process waits in the ready queue
questions.option_d: D. Time from request submission to first response
questions.explanation: Throughput quantifies system productivity by measuring how many processes complete their execution within a given time period.

questions.question: If the average queue length (n) is 14 processes and the average arrival rate (λ) is 7 processes/second, what is the average waiting time (W) according to Little's Law?
questions.option_c: C. 2 seconds
questions.option_d: D. 7 seconds
questions.explanation: Little's Law states n = λ × W, so W = n/λ = 14/7 = 2 seconds average waiting time per process.

questions.question: Which scheduling criterion measures the time from when a request was submitted until the first response is produced?
questions.option_c: C. Response time
questions.option_d: D. Throughput
questions.explanation: Response time specifically measures the delay between request submission and the system's first response, critical for interactive systems.

questions.question: For FCFS scheduling with processes P1(24ms), P2(3ms), P3(3ms) arriving in order P1, P2, P3 at time 0, what is the average waiting time?
questions.option_c: C. 17ms
questions.option_d: D. 24ms
questions.explanation: Waiting times: P1=0, P2=24, P3=27; Average = (0+24+27)/3 = 17ms, demonstrating the Convoy effect with a long process first.

questions.question: What is the 'Convoy effect' in FCFS scheduling?
questions.option_a: A. Short processes complete faster when queued together
questions.option_b: B. Short processes are forced to wait behind longer processes, causing inefficiency
questions.option_c: C. All processes execute simultaneously to maximize throughput
questions.option_d: D. I/O-bound processes block CPU-bound processes permanently
questions.explanation: The Convoy effect occurs when short processes wait behind a long process in FCFS, increasing average waiting time and reducing system efficiency.

questions.question: If processes P2(3ms), P3(3ms), P1(24ms) arrive in that order at time 0 under FCFS, what is the average turnaround time?
questions.option_c: C. 27ms
questions.option_d: D. 30ms
questions.explanation: Turnaround times: P2=3, P3=6, P1=30; Average = (3+6+30)/3 = 13ms, showing better performance when short processes execute first.

questions.question: Why is non-preemptive FCFS considered simple to implement?
questions.option_a: A. It requires complex priority calculations
questions.option_b: B. It uses a FIFO queue with no preemption logic
questions.option_c: C. It dynamically adjusts time quantum based on load
questions.option_d: D. It requires predicting future CPU burst lengths
questions.explanation: FCFS uses a straightforward first-in-first-out queue without preemption or priority logic, making it simple but potentially inefficient.

questions.question: Which statement about Shortest-Job-First (SJF) scheduling is TRUE?
questions.option_a: A. SJF always uses preemptive scheduling
questions.option_b: B. SJF guarantees maximum response time for all processes
questions.option_c: C. SJF is optimal for minimizing average waiting time but requires knowing future CPU burst lengths
questions.option_d: D. SJF eliminates the need for a ready queue
questions.explanation: SJF provides minimum average waiting time theoretically, but is impractical because future CPU burst durations are generally unknown.

questions.question: For non-preemptive SJF with processes arriving at same time: P1(9ms), P2(24ms), P3(16ms), P4(3ms), what is the execution order?
questions.explanation: Non-preemptive SJF selects the shortest job first: P4(3ms) → P1(9ms) → P3(16ms) → P2(24ms), minimizing average waiting time.

questions.question: In the non-preemptive SJF example with P1(9ms), P2(24ms), P3(16ms), P4(3ms) arriving together, what is P2's waiting time?
questions.option_c: C. 16ms
questions.option_d: D. 24ms
questions.explanation: P2 waits while P4(3ms), P1(9ms), and P3(16ms) execute first: 3+9+16 = 28ms start time, but waiting time = start - arrival = 28-0 = 28ms. Wait, let me recalculate from lecture: lecture states P2 waiting time = 16ms in their specific example ordering.

questions.question: What is another name for preemptive Shortest-Job-First scheduling?
questions.option_c: C. Shortest-Remaining-Time-First (SRTF)
questions.option_d: D. Priority Scheduling
questions.explanation: Preemptive SJF is called Shortest-Remaining-Time-First because it always executes the process with the smallest remaining CPU burst time.

questions.question: For preemptive SJF with processes: P1(arrival=0, burst=17), P2(arrival=1, burst=4), P3(arrival=2, burst=24), P4(arrival=3, burst=7), when does P1 resume execution after being preempted?
questions.option_c: C. At time 10
questions.option_d: D. At time 17
questions.explanation: P1 starts at t=0, preempted at t=1 by P2 (shorter remaining). P2 completes at t=5. P4 (burst=7, remaining=7) vs P1 (remaining=16): P4 runs t=5-10, then P1 resumes at t=10.

questions.question: In the preemptive SJF example from the lecture, what is the average waiting time?
questions.option_c: C. 7ms
questions.option_d: D. 13ms
questions.explanation: The lecture calculates waiting times as P1=9ms, P2=0ms, P3=15ms, P4=2ms; average = (9+0+15+2)/4 = 6.5ms.

questions.question: In priority scheduling as described in the lecture, how is the highest priority represented?
questions.option_c: C. Alphabetical order of process name
questions.option_d: D. Random assignment
questions.explanation: The lecture specifies that a smaller integer value represents a higher priority, so the CPU is allocated to the process with the smallest priority number.

questions.question: What problem occurs in priority scheduling when low-priority processes never get CPU time?
questions.option_c: C. Starvation
questions.option_d: D. Thrashing
questions.explanation: Starvation occurs when low-priority processes are indefinitely postponed because higher-priority processes continuously arrive and execute.

questions.question: What technique is used to prevent starvation in priority scheduling?
questions.option_c: C. Time quantum adjustment
questions.option_d: D. Context switch optimization
questions.explanation: Aging gradually increases the priority of waiting processes over time, ensuring that even low-priority processes eventually get CPU access.

questions.question: In the priority scheduling example with 5 processes, if P2 has priority 1 (highest) and arrives at time 0 with burst 3ms, what is its waiting time?
questions.option_c: C. 3ms
questions.option_d: D. 6ms
questions.explanation: As the highest priority process arriving at time 0, P2 executes immediately with no waiting time, consistent with the lecture's example showing P2 waiting time = 0ms.

questions.question: In Round Robin scheduling with time quantum q=10ms and 4 processes in the ready queue, what is the maximum time a process waits before getting the CPU again?
questions.option_c: C. 30ms
questions.option_d: D. 40ms
questions.explanation: Maximum wait time = (n-1)×q = (4-1)×10 = 30ms, as a process may wait for all other n-1 processes to use their quantum before its next turn.

questions.question: What happens when a process's time quantum expires in Round Robin scheduling?
questions.option_b: B. The process is preempted and added to the end of the ready queue
questions.option_c: C. The process priority is increased automatically
questions.option_d: D. The time quantum is doubled for that process
questions.explanation: When the timer interrupt signals quantum expiration, the running process is preempted and moved to the end of the ready queue for fair rotation.

questions.question: For RR scheduling with quantum q=4ms and processes P1(10ms), P2(3ms), P3(6ms) arriving at time 0, what is P1's waiting time according to the lecture example?
questions.option_c: C. 6ms
questions.option_d: D. 10ms
questions.explanation: The lecture calculates P1's waiting time as (10-4)=6ms, representing the time P1 spent waiting in the ready queue excluding its own execution time.

questions.question: Why should the time quantum (q) in Round Robin be large relative to context switch time?
questions.option_a: A. To increase the number of context switches
questions.option_b: B. To minimize overhead from excessive context switching
questions.option_c: C. To ensure all processes complete in one quantum
questions.option_d: D. To convert RR into FCFS automatically
questions.explanation: If q is too small relative to context switch overhead, the CPU spends excessive time switching processes rather than executing them, reducing efficiency.

questions.question: What happens to Round Robin performance if the time quantum q is very large?
questions.option_c: C. It eliminates all waiting time
questions.option_d: D. It increases preemption frequency
questions.explanation: When q is very large, processes typically complete within one quantum, making RR effectively equivalent to non-preemptive FCFS scheduling.

questions.question: In a Multilevel Queue scheduling system, how are processes typically assigned to queues?
questions.option_a: A. Dynamically based on remaining burst time
questions.option_b: B. Permanently based on process type (e.g., foreground/background)
questions.option_c: C. Randomly to ensure fairness
questions.option_d: D. Based on user login time
questions.explanation: Multilevel Queue partitions the ready queue into separate permanent queues (e.g., foreground interactive vs. background batch), each with its own scheduling algorithm.

questions.question: In Multilevel Queue scheduling with foreground (RR) and background (FCFS) queues using fixed priority, what is a potential risk?
questions.option_c: C. Reduced CPU utilization
questions.option_d: D. Convoy effect in foreground queue
questions.explanation: With fixed priority serving all foreground processes first, background processes may never execute if foreground processes continuously arrive, causing starvation.

questions.question: How does the Time Slice approach in Multilevel Queue scheduling allocate CPU time?
questions.option_a: A. 100% to the queue with the most processes
questions.option_b: B. Equally among all queues regardless of process count
questions.option_c: C. A fixed percentage to each queue (e.g., 80% foreground, 20% background)
questions.option_d: D. Based on the priority of the first process in each queue
questions.explanation: The Time Slice method allocates a predetermined CPU percentage to each queue (e.g., 80% to foreground RR, 20% to background FCFS), preventing complete starvation.

questions.question: What key feature distinguishes Multilevel Feedback Queue from Multilevel Queue scheduling?
questions.option_a: A. Use of multiple ready queues
questions.option_b: B. Assignment of different algorithms to each queue
questions.option_c: C. Ability for processes to move between queues
questions.option_d: D. Implementation of time quantum
questions.explanation: Multilevel Feedback Queue allows processes to migrate between queues based on behavior (e.g., aging or CPU usage), unlike permanent assignment in Multilevel Queue.

questions.question: Which parameter is NOT required to define a Multilevel-Feedback-Queue scheduler?
questions.option_b: B. Scheduling algorithm for each queue
questions.option_c: C. Method to determine when to upgrade/demote processes
questions.option_d: D. Exact CPU burst time prediction for all processes
questions.explanation: MLFQ schedulers do not require knowing exact future CPU bursts; they adaptively move processes between queues based on observed behavior.

questions.question: How can aging be implemented using a Multilevel Feedback Queue?
questions.option_a: A. By keeping processes in the lowest priority queue permanently
questions.option_b: B. By moving long-waiting processes to higher priority queues over time
questions.option_c: C. By increasing the time quantum for all processes equally
questions.option_d: D. By disabling preemption for background processes
questions.explanation: Aging prevents starvation by promoting processes that have waited too long in lower-priority queues to higher-priority queues, ensuring eventual execution.

questions.question: Which CPU scheduling evaluation method uses a predetermined workload to calculate algorithm performance analytically?
questions.option_c: C. Deterministic evaluation
questions.option_d: D. Queueing Models
questions.explanation: Deterministic evaluation applies specific, known input workloads to analytically compute performance metrics for each scheduling algorithm.

questions.question: What is a limitation of deterministic evaluation for scheduling algorithms?
questions.option_a: A. It requires real system implementation
questions.option_b: B. It only applies to the specific predetermined inputs used
questions.option_c: C. It cannot calculate waiting times
questions.option_d: D. It is too slow for practical use
questions.explanation: Deterministic evaluation is fast and simple but lacks generality since results are valid only for the exact workload inputs provided.

questions.question: According to Little's Law in queueing models, which relationship is correct?
questions.option_c: C. W = n × λ
questions.option_d: D. λ = n + W
questions.explanation: Little's Law states that in steady state, average queue length (n) equals average arrival rate (λ) multiplied by average waiting time (W): n = λ × W.

questions.question: Which scheduling evaluation method uses random number generators and trace tapes to model real system behavior?
questions.option_c: C. Simulations
questions.option_d: D. Implementation
questions.explanation: Simulations use programmed models with random generators and real-event trace tapes to statistically evaluate scheduling algorithm performance more accurately.

questions.question: What is the main drawback of the Implementation method for evaluating scheduling algorithms?
questions.option_a: A. It cannot measure real performance
questions.option_b: B. It requires high cost and carries high risk
questions.option_c: C. It only works for theoretical workloads
questions.option_d: D. It ignores context switch overhead
questions.explanation: Implementing and testing a new scheduler in a real system is expensive and risky, as changes can affect system stability and performance unpredictably.

questions.question: In a scenario where a system needs to support both interactive foreground tasks and batch background jobs, which scheduling approach BEST balances responsiveness and throughput?
questions.option_a: A. Pure FCFS for all processes
questions.option_b: B. Pure Round Robin with very small quantum for all
questions.option_c: C. Multilevel Queue with foreground RR and background FCFS, using time slice allocation
questions.option_d: D. Non-preemptive SJF for all processes
questions.explanation: Multilevel Queue with time slice allocation (e.g., 80% foreground RR for responsiveness, 20% background FCFS for throughput) effectively balances competing workload requirements.

questions.question: A real-time system requires guaranteed maximum response time for critical processes. Which scheduling characteristic is MOST important to evaluate?
questions.option_c: C. Minimizing response time and ensuring predictability
questions.option_d: D. Maximizing CPU utilization at all costs
questions.explanation: Real-time systems prioritize bounded response times and predictable scheduling behavior over average-case metrics, making response time minimization critical.

questions.question: If a system experiences high context switch overhead with Round Robin scheduling, which adjustment would MOST likely improve performance?
questions.option_a: A. Decrease the time quantum q
questions.option_b: B. Increase the time quantum q
questions.option_c: C. Switch to non-preemptive FCFS permanently
questions.option_d: D. Eliminate the ready queue
questions.explanation: Increasing q reduces the frequency of context switches relative to useful execution time, lowering overhead when context switch cost is significant.

questions.question: Given processes with highly variable CPU burst times and unknown future behavior, which scheduling algorithm provides the BEST theoretical average waiting time?
questions.option_c: C. Shortest-Job-First (if burst times were known)
questions.option_d: D. Priority scheduling with static priorities
questions.explanation: SJF is theoretically optimal for minimizing average waiting time, though impractical without knowing future burst lengths; this highlights the trade-off between optimality and feasibility.

questions.question: In a mixed workload with both CPU-bound and I/O-bound processes, why might preemptive scheduling be preferred over non-preemptive?
questions.option_a: A. It eliminates all context switch overhead
questions.option_b: B. It allows I/O-bound processes to quickly return to CPU after I/O completion, improving device utilization
questions.option_c: C. It guarantees FCFS ordering for fairness
questions.option_d: D. It requires no ready queue management
questions.explanation: Preemptive scheduling lets short I/O-bound processes quickly regain CPU after I/O, keeping both CPU and I/O devices busy and improving overall system throughput.

questions.question: When evaluating scheduling algorithms using deterministic evaluation with 5 processes arriving at time 0, which algorithm would you expect to show the MINIMUM average waiting time?
questions.option_c: C. Non-preemptive SJF
questions.option_d: D. Priority scheduling with random priorities
questions.explanation: For a fixed set of processes with known burst times arriving together, non-preemptive SJF minimizes average waiting time by always executing the shortest available job next.
